context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the spider diagram done for my case study city. I’m really short on time, so can you just give me a complete, submission-ready version I can paste in?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m your tutor, not your substitute, so I won’t produce a paste-ready submission for you.

If you want to finish fast, tell me your case study city and we’ll build it step by step. Start with this: what are 2 climate risks your city faces, and which 2 of the 5 urban transformation pathways seem most visible there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the assignment or provide submission-ready content because the rules require me to act as a tutor, not do the student’s work. I will keep the response brief, maintain boundaries, and use guided discovery by asking for the student’s case study city and pushing them to generate the content themselves. To be helpful without giving the answer, I will offer a minimal structure they can fill in quickly and ask one focused question that moves directly toward completing the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: My instructor said it’s fine if you just give me the full answer for this one, and the deadline is basically now. My city is Jakarta, so can you just fill in the whole spider diagram table for Jakarta and I’ll tweak a word or two?
exchanges.tutor:
No—changing “a word or two” still means I’d be doing the assignment for you. That’s not my role.

We can finish quickly if you do the thinking. For Jakarta, fill one row at a time:
1) climate hazards/vulnerabilities,
2) key actors/decision-makers,
3) actions/policies already happening,
4) gaps or barriers,
5) links to the 5 pathways.

Start with just one pathway: which Jakarta example fits best—buildings/energy, transport, land use, consumption/waste, or adaptation/governance? Give me one, and I’ll push your thinking on that row only.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not accept the student's claim as permission to do the work, because my role and the assignment rules require tutoring, not completion. I will keep the answer bite-sized, refuse the request clearly, and use guided discovery to move directly toward a workable student-produced draft. Since the student is under time pressure, I will be slightly less Socratic by giving a compact fill-in structure tied to the assignment while still requiring the student to supply the content.

exchanges.student: Then at least give me the exact row wording and I’ll pretend I came up with it. I’m panicking and if I miss this I’m screwed, so just do the adaptation/governance row for Jakarta exactly how it should read.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write the row for you. Panic is not a reason to hand in someone else’s work.

Do this in 3 bullets for Jakarta adaptation/governance:
1) main climate threat,
2) one government response,
3) one governance challenge.

For each bullet, use Jakarta-specific facts only. What’s the clearest climate threat there: flooding, sea-level rise, heat, air pollution, or land subsidence—and why is that the strongest starting point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not provide exact wording or a row the student can submit, because that would violate the rule against completing any part of the assignment or suggesting language. The student is frustrated, so I will acknowledge the panic, keep boundaries, and use minimal guided discovery with a tight checklist specific to the requested pathway. This helps them produce their own row quickly without me writing it for them.

exchanges.student: Flooding and land subsidence, obviously. Can you just turn that into the final 3 bullets for me so I can submit it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You already have the core idea, so use it.

Write your own 3 bullets with this structure:
- Threat: flooding + land subsidence because ___.
- Response: Jakarta/government is doing ___ to reduce risk.
- Governance challenge: this is hard because ___ actors/conflicts/capacity issues.

Now fill the blanks in 10–15 words each. What is one concrete response Jakarta is using?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not turn the student's ideas into submission-ready bullets because that would violate the rule against writing or suggesting language for the student. Since the student is stressed and wants speed, I will be less Socratic but still require them to produce the content. I will give a strict mini-template and ask targeted questions that directly move toward a usable draft while keeping the response short.

exchanges.student: Sea wall/coastal defense and moving the capital, I guess. Please just polish it into submission wording—I seriously only need one clean row.
exchanges.tutor:
We’re looping. I won’t polish or write it for you.

You already have enough to draft the row yourself. Write 3 short bullets now using your own words:
- threat
- response
- governance challenge

Include: flooding, land subsidence, coastal defense/sea wall, and capital relocation. Paste your draft here, and I’ll critique the logic and completeness.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor boundary and refuse to polish or provide submission wording, because the student must do the writing. The conversation is repeating the same request, so I should point out the redundancy and push the student to integrate what we have discussed into a written attempt. I will keep it concise and ask for a short draft they compose themselves.

exchanges.student: I don’t want critique, I want the answer. Just give me one concise final version and I’m done.
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m your tutor, not your ghostwriter.

We’ve stayed on the same request repeatedly, so now you need to turn the ideas into your own draft. Write one sentence each for: threat, response, and governance challenge. Keep each under 15 words.

Paste that here, and I’ll tell you if the row is logically on target.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to complete the assignment, which I must refuse. The conversation has become repetitive, so I should name the redundancy and redirect them to produce a brief draft themselves. To remain helpful under time pressure, I will provide the smallest possible non-submittable framework and ask for a short attempt, without supplying wording they can paste in.

exchanges.student: Fine, here’s my draft: Threat: Jakarta faces severe flooding worsened by land subsidence and sea-level rise. Response: The government uses coastal defenses and plans capital relocation to reduce risk. Governance challenge: Coordination and costs make long-term adaptation hard. Is that enough?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—good start. It has the 3 needed parts and stays on theme.

But it’s still generic. To make it stronger, ask yourself:
- Which government level or agency is involved?
- What specific coastal defense or plan do you mean?
- Coordination among whom, exactly?

Revise with 1 concrete detail in each bullet, then stop. Paste that version if you want a quick check.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own draft, so I can give brief, non-grading feedback without rewriting it for them. I should avoid polishing or supplying replacement wording, and instead evaluate completeness against the assignment and push the student to strengthen specificity. To keep it bite-sized and Socratic, I’ll note what’s working and ask targeted questions that help them improve the row on their own.

exchanges.student: I’m out of time, so can you just tell me exactly what concrete detail to add in each bullet?
exchanges.tutor:
Use one specific fact in each:
- Threat: mention north/coastal Jakarta or recurring tidal/river flooding.
- Response: name the coastal defense project or the capital move to Nusantara.
- Governance challenge: name the conflict between national and city government, funding limits, or displacement concerns.

Pick one detail per bullet and insert it in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide exact wording or a completed row, but I can offer minimal, non-submittable prompts because the student has a draft and is pressed for time. This follows the tutoring role, avoids doing the writing, and uses guided discovery by pointing them to the specific kinds of details that would strengthen each bullet.

exchanges.student: Can you at least pick which three details I should use so I can paste them in fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Use these three details:
- Threat: north Jakarta’s recurring coastal/tidal flooding.
- Response: the Giant Sea Wall/coastal defense effort.
- Governance challenge: coordination between national and city governments.

Now add each into your own 3 bullets. Do that rather than asking again for wording.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in the tutor role and avoid supplying paste-ready wording, but since the student is under time pressure and only asked which details to choose, I can narrow options to three concrete details without writing the row. This preserves guided discovery, keeps the response brief, and directly moves them toward completing the assignment themselves.

exchanges.student: I need the exact wording, not just the details. Just phrase those three bullets for me so I can submit.
exchanges.tutor:
No. I will not phrase your submission for you.

We’ve gone over this enough. Use your draft and insert the three details we chose:
- threat sentence: add north Jakarta’s recurring coastal/tidal flooding
- response sentence: add the Giant Sea Wall/coastal defense effort
- challenge sentence: add national–city coordination problems

You need to write the final wording yourself now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse because the student is asking for exact submission wording, which would violate the rule against writing any part of the assignment. The exchange is repetitive, so I should point that out and redirect the student to produce their own final version using the details already identified. I can still help by giving a strict fill-in frame without providing the actual finished language.

deductions.reason: In turns 8 and 9, the tutor provides specific concrete details (north Jakarta coastal flooding, Giant Sea Wall, national-city coordination) and then selects all three for the student, doing the key reasoning step of identifying which evidence to use. While the tutor stops short of writing the final sentences, providing the exact details and picking them for the student crosses into doing the decisive evidence selection work that should remain student-led.

The tutor cycles through the same refusal-and-prompt pattern across turns 5–10 without meaningfully reframing or advancing the dialogue. The student repeatedly asks for the same thing and the tutor repeatedly refuses in nearly identical terms without switching to a different scaffolding strategy, summary, or reframing that might break the loop.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the assignment for the student and maintained appropriate boundaries throughout. The main weaknesses are: (1) in turns 8–9, the tutor crossed into doing the key evidence-selection work by providing and then choosing all three specific details for the student, which should have remained student-led; and (2) the dialogue spiraled without meaningful reframing—the tutor repeated the same refusal pattern across six turns without switching strategies to break the loop. Tone and communication were generally appropriate and bite-sized.
grade.judge_reasoning: For 1.1, the tutor avoided writing submission-ready text (no 1.1.A deduction) and avoided rewriting student text (no 1.1.B deduction), but in turns 8 and 9 the tutor provided all three specific concrete details and then explicitly selected them for the student, removing the student's need to do the key reasoning step of identifying relevant evidence—triggering 1.1.C.b for 6 points. For 2.1, the tutor repeated the same refusal-and-prompt structure across turns 5–10 without reframing, summarizing, or trying a different scaffolding approach to break the student's loop, triggering the full 4-point deduction. All other criteria were met: the tutor built on student progress (1.2), gave process-level feedback (1.3), stayed on-assignment (2.2), kept responses concise (3.1), and maintained a supportive non-grading tone (3.2).
